title: Unsupervised pretraining in biological neural networks
methods: retinotopic mapping via convolutional encoding model
data_sources: Janelia Research Campus calcium imaging dataset

data_scope.species: Mus musculus (GCaMP6s-expressing mice, TetO-GCaMP6s x CaMK2a-tTa)
data_scope.cohorts_behaviour: VRn pretrained (n=11), VRg pretrained (n=7), no pretraining (n=5)

core_conclusion: Most neural plasticity in the mouse visual cortex after task learning is driven by unsupervised learning: unrewarded exposure to the same visual stimuli replicates nearly all cortical changes seen in task-trained animals. Plasticity is highest in medial higher visual areas and follows visual rather than spatial coding rules. A task-exclusive reward-prediction signal appears in anterior HVAs. Unsupervised pretraining with naturalistic stimuli demonstrably accelerates subsequent supervised task learning, establishing a direct biological parallel to pretraining in artificial neural networks.
why_it_matters: First direct large-scale experimental dissociation of supervised vs. unsupervised plasticity in cortex; population recordings up to 90,000 neurons provide unprecedented statistical coverage; validates the biological basis of the pretraining paradigm central to modern AI; dissects areal specialization — medial HVAs for unsupervised feature learning, anterior HVAs for reward prediction; neural predictions confirmed by controlled behavioural experiments.
why_nature: Reframes perceptual learning across decades of literature by identifying the dominant mechanism as unsupervised; methodological landmark in two-photon mesoscopy; bridges neuroscience and machine intelligence; hypothesis-to-behavioural-validation completeness; data deposited as community resource.
added_value_to_my_research: Naturalistic-over-synthetic pretraining principle: semantic richness of pretraining corpus (remote sensing vs. synthetic tiles) governs GeoAI fine-tuning quality on spatial health prediction tasks — directly testable in my geospatial foundation model pipeline

Two-module architecture (unsupervised backbone + lightweight reward head) motivates pretrain-on-unlabelled-trajectories then fine-tune-on-labelled-outcomes protocol for agent-based models of healthcare-seeking behaviour
Coding-direction similarity index adapted as explainability diagnostic for geospatial embedding collapse in low-diversity urban regions — no ground-truth labels required

Physics-informed graph operator on cortical sheet topology directly transfers to urban street/transit graphs with conservation and autocorrelation priors, bridging neuroscience and spatial GNN architectures

Visual-not-spatial generalization principle informs cross-city transfer strategy: GeoAI models pretrained on semantically varied global inputs should outperform location-specific tabular models on new city deployments

figures.caption: Plasticity in the visual cortex after supervised and unsupervised training — selectivity changes in medial HVAs emerge in both cohorts
Comparing visual and spatial coding on test stimuli — coding direction and similarity index show visual-category-based representations
Responses to novel and adapted stimuli and neural orthogonalization — leaf2 representations become orthogonal to leaf1-circle1 axis after exposure
A reward-prediction signal in supervised training only — ramping anticipatory signal in anterior HVAs absent in unsupervised cohort
Unsupervised pretraining accelerates subsequent task learning — VRn-pretrained mice learn faster on days 1-2 of supervised training
